Although I do not believe margins will finish at 7% or 12%, this just shows the variability for the business. Even though it is slightly undervalued by my model, HII is not a buy due to the major risks it faces that other military contractors do not, thus requiring a larger margin of safety before I would consider purchasing shares.

Risks And Final Thoughts

As mentioned above, the biggest risks are macro-related (supply chain disruptions, inflationary pressures, workforce challenges) and margin volatility. Given the long-term and often fixed nature of their government contracts, HII takes inflationary and workforce risks, which, as we saw, can destroy margins. Unless contract structures move more toward cost-reimbursement contracts, margins will continue to be vulnerable. Additionally, finding skilled laborers could also be a challenge moving forward, which may increase production costs and build times. Secondly, defense spending may be cut. Trump’s shipbuilding plans started the HII stock rally, but they have not materialized and are under threat as Congress is more focused on other portions of the military, as well as overall budget deficit concerns. If we do not see global tensions rise, it is hard to find the urge to increase spending, which could put management’s $15 billion revenue target under threat. Having revenue dependent on government spending makes HII’s business countercyclical, but it also makes it vulnerable to budget cuts, especially given HII’s shipbuilding concentration.

Although HII is slightly undervalued, I would hold off purchasing shares unless the stock declines, more shipbuilding funding is granted, or an increase in global tensions occurs. Based on what I know today, I would consider entering a position if shares come back to around the $200-$210 range, representing about a 20%-25% margin of safety compared to my intrinsic value estimate; therefore, I am giving HII a Hold rating.

data.title: Alphabet: Fortunes Could Be Lost
data.description:
Alphabet's (GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) shares trade at a similar level at which they were trading in November when our latest article on the business was submitted. Since that time, the risks that the company faces have only increased, and they now threaten to disrupt its business model in the following years. Alphabet's biggest problem at this point is that it faces major regulatory pressure from antitrust watchdogs in the United States and the European Union.

The materialization of such pressure could lead to fines that could cost the company a fortune or even result in the breakup of some parts of Alphabet's business. In addition to that, Alphabet faces increased competitive pressure, which can also make it harder for its business to grow in the future. For those and other reasons that will be highlighted in this article, we decided to continue to rate Alphabet as a SELL.

Risks At Home Are On The Rise

To this day, Alphabet continues to heavily rely on its search business, which generates the most revenue for the business. In Q1, the search business generated $46.16 billion in revenues, which is more than half of the total revenues of $80.54 billion during the period. Such reliance on one particular business not only has its upsides but also downsides.

In the United States, Alphabet has been accused of having an illegal stronghold in markets for publisher ad servers and the market for ad exchanges. As a result of this, the Department of Justice wants the company to get rid of its ad exchange and ad server businesses, which could weaken Alphabet's dominance over the search industry. The remedies trial is scheduled to take place on September 22.

In a separate case, Alphabet has been accused of holding a monopoly in search and using unlawful tactics to maintain it. The government, among other things, now wants Alphabet to sell its Chrome browser and ban the company from paying Apple to have Google remain as the main search engine in Safari browser. The federal judge is expected to make the final decision about remedies in August.

If, in the end, Alphabet is forced to do most of the things that the government wants, then its search business will be at risk of a major disruption. This is because owning the Chrome browser and having an exclusive deal with Apple have been helping Alphabet dominate the search industry in the last decade. Without all of that, it could lose its advantages and see its major source of revenue underperform in the future.

The Risk Of Disruption In Europe Is Real

At the same time, in the European Union, Alphabet is also facing major pressure from regulators. In addition to probably losing the appeal and paying the €4.124 billion fine for breaching EU antitrust rules a few years ago, the company in March was also accused of breaching the Digital Markets Act for two of its services. While the company offered to make some changes to how it shows search results, Reuters has reported that some of the rivals were dissatisfied with the current offering.

If, in the end, the European Commission is dissatisfied with what Alphabet is offering, it could fine the company up to 10% of its global turnover for breaching the Digital Markets Act. Considering that under the EU antitrust law, turnover basically refers to the sale of products and services, excluding VAT and other duties, it's possible that the European Commission could impose an up to 10% fine on its annual revenues. Since in FY24 Alphabet's revenues were $350 billion, a 10% fine will translate to $35 billion, which is more than a third of its overall cash reserves at the end of Q1.

Considering all of this, it's not an exaggeration to say that fortunes could be lost and Alphabet's whole business is currently at risk of a major disruption due to the regulatory pressure both in the United States and the European Union.

The Competition Is On The Rise

Even if we exclude the regulatory risks, Alphabet's leadership position in the search business is still under threat. In May, it was revealed that searches on Apple's (AAPL) Safari browser, where Google is the primary search engine, are declining. In part, this is because of the rise of AI chatbots that are emerging as a serious threat to Alphabet's dominant position in search. What's more is that Apple is now also looking to integrate AI chatbots into its ecosystem and could strike deals with companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, or Perplexity to do so. This could indicate that the era of traditional search is coming to an end.

Last month, it was also announced that Apple has been thinking about buying Perplexity AI, which could be a sign that the Cupertino giant might be planning to develop its internal AI-based search engine. In addition to that, a couple of weeks ago, it was reported that OpenAI is about to launch its own browser, which would be competing directly with Alphabet's Chrome.

All of those developments show that even if Alphabet manages to mitigate regulatory risks, its leadership position in the search business is under threat of disruption.

There's Still Some Time Left

It's still possible that Alphabet will be able to avoid a worst-case scenario, at least in the short to near term. While Alphabet will probably lose its appeal and pay a €4.124 billion fine for breaching EU antitrust rules, the fine itself was imposed in 2018, while the Court is yet to make a final decision. This shows that even if the European Commission imposes another fine for breaching the Digital Markets Act in the latest case, Alphabet will likely appeal it, and it could take up to a decade for the Court to come to a final decision.

The same thing could happen in the United States, where the company already stated that it will likely appeal an upcoming ruling of the federal judge. Considering that Microsoft (MSFT) avoided a spin-off in 2001 by appealing the initial decision to split the business and later settling the case with the Department of Justice, it's possible that Alphabet will also be able to avoid the harshest penalties using this precedent. That's why, in the short to near term, the risks of major disruption caused by regulatory actions will likely be less significant.

In the meantime, Alphabet might continue to show a decent performance in the following quarters. It already performed well in Q1, where its revenues exceeded expectations by over $1 billion. The Q2 results are expected to be released next week, and the latest forecasts suggest that the business will likely grow its revenues and earnings at a double-digit rate as well.

In addition to that, to mitigate the competitive risks, Alphabet has also been expanding its AI portfolio of products and services. Its Gemini chatbot continues to gain traction and has an average of almost 400 million monthly visits, while its AI Overviews feature within Google's search engine reached 1.5 billion monthly visits last quarter. The company has also been awarded the AI-related contract by the Department of Defense earlier this month, which shows that its AI products could be competitive against other AI chatbots and could make it possible for Alphabet to remain the dominant name in the search business. Also, the increased popularity of YouTube and the rising monetization from Shorts could help Alphabet's video business perform better than expected and help the overall business offset some of the potential losses from the developments described earlier in this article.

The Intrinsic Value of Alphabet

Considering all the upsides and downsides, we decided to create a valuation model to find out Alphabet's intrinsic value. In our model, we forecast the company's performance for the next five years and use a 3% perpetual growth rate, which is not that far away from the historical GDP and inflation rate. We also created the model when Alphabet was trading at $183.63 per share, while the cash and debt numbers have been taken from the recent earnings report.

We also used the discount rate of 7.71%. We calculated it by finding out Alphabet's cost of debt and cost of equity rates. For the cost of debt calculations, the total debt numbers have been taken from the latest earnings report, while the numbers for the remaining metrics are from the trailing twelve months. For the cost of equity calculations, we used a risk-free rate of 4.40%, a beta of 1.01, and the market return rate of 7.77%.

Back in November, our previous model assumed a sales growth rate in 2025 of slightly above 11%, after which we expected a gradual decrease in the annual growth rate to 10% by the terminal year. However, considering that Alphabet received dozens of downward revisions recently due to the rising risks and the changing macro environment, the latest estimates assume a sales growth rate of slightly less than 11% in 2025. Considering this, we also decreased our sales expectations, which are slightly lower than before but also not that far away from the latest expectations.

The EBIT margin in the model is the same as the year ago, and we don't expect it to decrease for now, since the bottom-line performance in Q1 was above expectations, and the company received major upward EPS revisions lately. The tax rate in the model is 21%, which is the current standard corporate rate in the United States. The assumptions for the D&A are in line with the historical performance, while the assumptions for the change in NWC are similar to our previous model.

The CapEx assumptions in the model have been greatly increased. In 2025, we assume a CapEx of around $75 billion, in line with the management's latest expectations.

The updated model under new assumptions shows that Alphabet's enterprise value is $1.53 trillion, while the equity value is $1.6 trillion. That translates to an intrinsic value of $129.78 per share, below the current market price by around 29%.

Final Thoughts

In our opinion, Alphabet is already overvalued at the current price, even if we exclude the potential fines or the breakup of some parts of its business. If those risks also materialize, Alphabet could lose a fortune and also no longer have important income sources, which would lead to a lower equity value and the subsequent intrinsic value. Considering this, we don't think that Alphabet's stock is a solid investment and continue to rate it as a SELL, especially since the shares haven't moved much since we last covered the company in November.

data.title: QQQI: The Fund's Strategy Has Again Proven To Be Successful In A Changing Market Environment
data.description:
Often times, sacrifice is part of investing. While occasionally a company or fund offers investors both consistent growth and income, people usually have to choose what they value most when they allocate capital. Some people will be more comfortable taking on higher risks to get potentially stronger growth, while others will focus on income-based investments that offer more stability, but often at the cost of additional upside potential.

There has been a lot of evolution in dividend and income-based investing since 2008, since low rates have forced many traditionally risk-averse investors into equities and investment funds. While the standard way of thinking was that exchange-traded investments focused on income, such as covered-call funds, would not be able to capture all or most of the upside of the equities and indexes these financials track, some managers have developed strategies recently that offer individuals consistent payouts and substantive total returns as well.

The new-to-market NEOS funds have consistently been some of the strongest-performing covered call investments in the market. The NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI) has been one of the best funds to use these options-based strategies.

The NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF has offered investors total returns of 31.99 percent since the fund's inception in January of 2024, while the underlying Nasdaq-100 index that this investment tracks has offered investors total returns of 33.37 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF in April of 2025, and I wanted to update my coverage since this fund only recently came to market. Today, I am reiterating my strong buy rating of this fund. QQQI has performed very well in both more adverse conditions and during recent rallies. The managers of this ETF have shown the ability to effectively adjust the options strategies to changing and more difficult market conditions. The investment's recent outperformance of the Nasdaq-100 index, which this ETF closely tracks, shows in rising market conditions further highlights the fund's ability to outperform the benchmark during rallies as well.

The NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF has an expense ratio of 0.68 percent, $3.05 billion in assets under management, and a trailing yield of 13.85 percent. This investment tracks the Nasdaq-100. QQQI is invested 54.32 percent in technology, 15.61 percent in communication companies, 12.80 percent in consumer cyclicals, 5.16 percent in consumer defensives, 4.88 percent in health care, 3.45 percent in industrials, 1.42 percent in utilities, 1.27 percent in basic materials, 0.45 percent in energy, 0.43 percent in financials, and 0.20 percent in real estate. The fund's largest holdings are NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), and Broadcom (AVGO). These positions make up nearly 30 percent of the overall ETF's assets. This fund rebalances quarterly and reconstitutes annually.

The formula that the managers of QQQI use, which is similar to the approach taken by most of the NEOS funds, is a two-step option strategy. The first part of this approach is to sell NDX calls against the equity positions the fund holds; the managers have the discretion to determine to what extent these options are sold and at what strike price the option contracts are written at each month. The second part of the options strategy is to use income from the covered calls that are sold to buy out-of-the-money calls. This ETF sells NDX options, which are classified as 1256 contracts and taxed at a 60 percent long-term capital gains rate and 40 percent ordinary income rate, so this investment offers some tax advantages as well. This investment sells covered calls and credit call spreads.

The covered-call funds that sell out-of-the-money calls that have the discretion to decide both how many contracts these funds sell against assets and what the strike price is for these options are the covered-calls ETFs that have performed the best over the last several years. The NEOs funds and Goldman Sachs' new covered-calls funds, the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), have consistently used more flexible options-based strategies to outperform more traditional covered-call funds such as the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) and the Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD), which do not adjust these funds' approach even as market conditions change.

GPIX and QQQI have both significantly outperformed QYLD despite also tracking and being exposed to the same Nasdaq-100 index.

Even though QQQI has limited data because the fund's inception date was in January of 2024, this fund consistently outperformed the Nasdaq-100 index both during the more volatile market conditions in March and April, as well as more recently when the equities have rallied since early May. This NEOS fund's income has also stayed steady even during periods of heightened volatility.

NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF has paid out between $.58 a share and nearly $.64 a share over the last 6 months. While some investors may have expected more income with the volatility levels higher, the managers of this NEOS fund adjust the strike price options sold to market conditions so payouts are consistent even in a challenging environment. The primary goal of this investment is consistent and substantive income, not maximizing total returns or payouts, and if the fund tried to take advantage of rising volatility levels to generate additional income, that would also create larger risks for the principal since the options written likely would not be close to at-the-money despite market sell-offs often being more pronounced.

All investments have risks, and this NEOS fund has less than 2 years of data. Still, the strategy this investment uses has been shown to be effective when utilized correctly by other funds over a more extended period of time. Also, this ETF has still slightly underperformed the Nasdaq-100 index since the fund's inception, even though QQQI has outperformed this benchmark more recently. Covered-call funds are income-based investments. QQQI is still not likely to outperform the broader indexes over the long term, even though this ETF should continue to offer both consistent payouts and total returns. QQQI has also not had to operate during a prolonged sell-off, either, although the investment outperformed in March and April of this year when the indexes sold off hard because of some political and economic uncertainty. Earnings estimates or large-cap tech remain strong as well, with AI expected to be a main driver for the sector moving forward. The consensus amongst analysts is for large-cap tech to grow earnings by nearly 18 percent in 2026.

While financial goals often remain the same over long periods of time, investing strategies should adjust to changing market conditions and better investment options that are available. When most original covered-call funds became more common, these investments used fairly simple and more rigid strategies. The newer options-based ETFs have likely incorporated data from previous investments to develop more sophisticated and flexible approaches, and the NEOS funds have successfully been able to use a more nuanced strategy that the managers have effectively applied in a variety of even more challenging market environments.

data.title: Nvidia: Back In China? This Could Be The Final Big Hurrah
data.description:
Nvidia: H20 AI Chips To China Gets "Ok". What's Next?

Jensen Huang always seems to have upside surprises up his sleeves, and I wonder what else he might unveil when NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) reports its second fiscal quarter earnings at the end of August. As the CEO of the world's most valuable company by market cap, I expressed my doubts in my previous NVDA writeup that Huang could still conjure enough magic to keep investor sentiments from waning.

Yet, the anticipated restoration of Nvidia's H20 AI chips business for its Chinese customers has elevated near-term growth prospects for the company. In other words, we should expect Nvidia's full-year guidance to be upgraded, reversing some of the previous writedowns attributed to the Chinese-related chips business. Bloomberg Intelligence even projected that the move to resume H20 chips sales in China could help the semiconductor leader regain a substantive share of the $15B in data center revenue for the current fiscal year, attributed to the chip export controls.

Yet, objections from the China hawks in the US Congress suggest we must be careful about taking the current policy stance as "final", given the significant policy upheavals experienced under the Trump administration. While the frosty relations with China appear to have thawed as both sides plausibly work toward a Trump-Xi summit, policy imperatives based on the nature of transactional diplomacy (chips for rare earths?) could also be shifted.

In other words, I urge investors to be circumspect about triangulating the positive developments on the H20 to a longer-term structural shift in America's overarching position on China. China is assessed to be highly determined to not just keep pace but also aims to surpass America's AI primacy.

While the H20 chips are not expected to help China's AI companies leapfrog the US as the forerunner of the most cutting-edge AI models, China's aggressive pursuit of the development of open-source AI models could still spur wider adoption of its models outside the US. Nevertheless, I concur that the near-term benefits to Nvidia's operating performance and outlook should be keenly felt when the company reports quarterly earnings in August.

Nvidia: AI Growth Rates Called Into Question

Yet, could this also be the "final (big) hurrah" that we might see from Huang for some time? Sure, I know Nvidia's drumming up the Sovereign AI theme to maintain gargantuan expectations on the company's growth trajectory.

Accordingly, Nvidia's data center revenue surged 100% in the past twelve months, accounting for more than 88% of its total revenue base. Having a stranglehold on the market share of highly in-demand AI chips for the top cloud computing companies and AI developers has driven unprecedented success for Huang and his team.

However, it's not an enigma that such substantial growth rates shouldn't be expected to persist, as the law of large numbers eventually returns to haunt overoptimistic Nvidia investors. Accordingly, Nvidia's growth profile is expected to continue decelerating rather quickly through FY2027. Doesn't that leave critical questions about what FY2028's growth rate could look like? Shouldn't we also anticipate considerable variability in forecasts while the Sovereign AI themes play out? Doesn't that imply that we should be careful about pricing in an even higher valuation upgrade/re-rating for NVDA?

And, I don't think I need to remind you that while Nvidia's top customers (America's big tech) stuff themselves with Nvidia's GPUs to push the AI frontier, expecting the current growth curve to continue indefinitely isn't reasonable or possible. As investors, we must contemplate that, unless the Sovereign AI themes could take off quickly, Nvidia's AI opportunity could also face similar digestion risks as the US hyperscalers work through their substantially upgraded capacity.

Nvidia's Sovereign AI Themes May Not Garner Similar Traction

To assess the cadence on the Sovereign AI themes, we need to examine the interests of government-led initiatives outside the US to consider whether they strive/commit to investing as aggressively as the US tech companies. I'm sure Huang understands that he cannot solely depend on the growth/recovery of China tech's demand to afford the company a reliable pillar of support. The vicissitudes underlining the geopolitical intricacies of US-China relations could complicate such prospects amidst their battle for AI supremacy. In a recent dialogue, Jensen Huang added on to his conviction about the Sovereign AI lever that he's clearly priming to undertake as Nvidia's next most pivotal growth vector to rejuvenate the potential topline deceleration. Huang:

...Every country should also build its own indigenous AI stack and its AI models. And that AI model is trained on their language, their history, the knowledge of their society, their culture, and their values. It’s not sensible that one Western company will be able to capture and somehow deeply appreciate the values of every country, every religion, and every background… A Conversation with Jensen Huang (from 25:02 onward)

Huang's message is apt. And I believe he believes Sovereign AI could become a critical differentiator for the respective national AI champions as AI capabilities continue to diffuse around the world. Yet, even Singapore, which reportedly is No. 1 in the world in terms of per-capita usage of ChatGPT, doesn't seem perturbed at not being at the leading or cutting edge when it comes to building frontier AI models. In contrast, Singapore's leaders appear ready to adopt the current models for its national strategy, even mentioning "DeepSeek and other things". As Singapore's Senior Minister Lee Hsien Loong (former Prime Minister) highlighted in a recent dialogue:

Even if I’m not at the leading edge generating the next DeepSeek, I should be able to make use of DeepSeek and other things, in order to do our jobs better, deliver services better, be more efficient, and be more productive. And I think that is within Singaporeans’ capabilities, and we can train Singaporeans to be able to adapt to this. - SM Lee Hsien Loong’s Dialogue at the 69th Economic Society of Singapore Annual Dinner (from 44:35 onward)

The policy stance is possibly skewed toward each country's AI capabilities or comparative AI advantages. It's no secret that a significant percentage of the world's elite AI talents are situated in China (50% of global AI developers are based in China, according to Jensen Huang). And I believe we can also find many of them in Silicon Valley if we consider the exodus of talent that formed Meta Superintelligence Labs (META) recently?

Hence, other countries may need to be realistic about their strategic AI objectives. That might mean deploying resources on applying AI pragmatically to uplift the AI skill sets of their citizens, as compared to focusing their attention on building cutting-edge Sovereign AI models. As Lee underscored about focusing on "understanding and using [AI]," pointing to the practicality of applied AI. Makes sense? Hence, I believe we must be very careful when trying to parlay what Jensen Huang believes in and what other countries might be contemplating.

Buy Or Sell NVDA Stock?

What does that mean for Nvidia? I believe it's increasingly clear that the market could reallocate to AMD (AMD) as the Lisa Su-led company starts gaining traction in reigniting its AI growth prospects, as I expounded in a recent AMD article.

And from the NVDA/AMD price chart above, we can also correlate that NVDA's outperformance over AMD appears to have stagnated, going through a consolidation zone over the past six months. The last time a similar occurrence panned out was back in late 2023, before NVDA regained its upward traction and momentum against AMD.

However, we are no longer in early 2024. Nvidia's growth rates are also expected to continue falling substantially through FY2027 and possibly beyond, even as it seeks a new growth arena in Sovereign AI.

It remains too nascent and packed with sizable uncertainties when weighing in on delivering a significantly bullish prognosis. NVDA's forward EBITDA multiple of 29x appears to be discernibly under its 10Y average of 34x. However, I think the market isn't foolish. Investors are arguably adjusting to the structural growth deceleration in Nvidia's outlook and the ambivalent Sovereign AI growth themes that are needed to spur the next growth inflection on NVDA's bullish narrative.

While the unexpected H20 AI chips approval seems to have uplifted near-term market sentiments on the stock, it could also mark NVDA's final hurrah before a prolonged digestion/consolidation phase follows through subsequently.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Bank7: An Upgrade Is Justified After This Strong Showing
data.description:
July 18th ended up being a really good day for shareholders of Bank7 (NASDAQ:BSVN). Shares of the company closed up 4.5% after management announced financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Although profitability declined on a year over year basis, revenue and earnings per share did come in higher than what analysts anticipated. The company continues to see growth in deposits and asset quality remains top tier. With this move higher, the stock is now up 25.4% since I reaffirmed it as a ‘hold’ candidate back in May of this year. That significantly outpaces the 7.3% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time.

Looking back at the data today, I'm honestly a little disappointed in myself. The firm continues to outperform my own expectations. Even though the net interest margin is declining, the trajectory of the company in other respects is positive. While the price to earnings multiple of the company is not absurd by any means, and is actually near the lower end of the spectrum compared to other similar banks, I was turned off by its high price to book and high price to tangible book multiples. In retrospect, I think I was overly conservative with this name. And even though a lot of the easy money has been made by now, I think that upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ is probably the right choice.

A solid quarter

After the market closed on July 17th, the management team at Bank7 announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. The quarter for the company was defined, in part, by continued growth in key areas of the balance sheet. Overall deposits, for instance, hit $1.59 billion. That's up from the $1.55 billion the company had at the end of the first quarter of this year, and it compared favorably to the $1.52 billion the company had in 2024. One thing that I really like about the institution is that, compared to many of the other banks that I analyze, it has fairly low uninsured deposit exposure. At the end of the most recent quarter, it had $380.3 million, or 23.9%, of total deposits classified as uninsured. This is below the 30% maximum threshold that I like to see. Management described core deposit growth as organic and significant in nature, with this attributed to its bankers.

Other aspects of the balance sheet deserve attention also. As deposits have increased, the value of loans on its books has risen as well. In 2024, the company had loans of $1.38 billion. These grew to $1.41 billion in the first quarter of this year before climbing further to $1.48 billion in the second quarter of 2025. The largest chunk of the company's loan portfolio would be the $366.6 million dedicated to commercial and industrial properties. That's about 24.4% of its total loan portfolio. Hospitality properties that fall under operational commercial real estate come in second place at 18.5%. Other major areas of exposure would be non-owner occupied commercial real estate, one to four family commercial properties, and commercial construction loans, at 10.2%, 8.4%, and 9.1%, respectively. Only about $79.7 million, or 5.3%, of its loan portfolio involves office assets. This is refreshing to me since office assets are quite risky right now because of high and rising vacancy rates.

Most other aspects of the balance sheet are fairly small. The company ended the quarter with just $57.2 million dedicated to securities. It did have more cash, but that stood at just $233 million. That was down from the $253.5 million the company had in the first quarter, and it compared negatively to the $240.9 million reported for 2024. I don't really have much of an issue here, however, because the company has no debt on its books and is clearly transferring more of its capital toward loans. Speaking of loans, during the first quarter earnings call, management mentioned that their pipeline for loans remains strong heading into the third quarter. That is certainly encouraging.

When it comes to the income statement, we had some interesting surprises. During the quarter, net interest income for the company was $21.7 million. That's up from the $21.2 million reported the previous year. This is in spite of the fact that the company's net interest margin contracted from 5.15% to 4.96%. It was driven, then, by a rise in the size of the firm's balance sheet. Even though we did see this increase year over year, for the first half of the year as a whole, that $42.6 million generated by the company did come in lower than the $43.2 million reported the previous year. It doesn't help here that the company suffered from a drop in its net interest margin from 5.15% to 4.97%.

Non-interest income ended up contracting year over year from $3.2 million to $2.7 million. This was in spite of the fact that the company saw a surge in mortgage lending income from $78,000 to $0.5 million. It appears as though the drop was driven by a fall in other income from $2.8 million to $1.9 million. However, we don't know exactly what that entailed. Despite this drop, total net revenue for the company for the quarter came in at $24.4 million, beating analysts’ expectations by $1.5 million.

When it comes to profitability, we did see a year over year drop because of the lower non-interest income and because of a rise in non-interest expense thanks in large part to greater salaries and employee benefits costs. Overall earnings per share dropped from $1.23 last year to $1.16 this year, taking net profits down from $11.5 million to $11.1 million. As disappointing as this was, the firm still exceeded analysts’ expectations on the bottom line by $0.16 per share.

If we assume that the rest of this year will look like the first half of this year, we would get net profits for 2025 of $42.9 million. Using this estimate, we can see in the chart above that the company is trading at a price to earnings multiple of 10.6. This is only slightly above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. However, when stacked up against five other similar firms as shown in that chart, only one of the five companies is cheaper than it is. In the chart below, I did the same type of analysis using the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. This is where the company is very expensive on both an absolute basis and relative to other firms. In fact, it is more expensive than any of the five companies I compared it to.

One very positive thing about the company is that its asset quality remains top notch. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets, which come in at 2.47%. This is well above the 1% minimum that I like to see. It also places the company above any of the other five banks that I compared it to. In the subsequent chart, you can see a return on equity reading of 19.62%. This is almost double the 10% minimum threshold that I like to see. It also makes the company superior to the other five firms I compared it to.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I honestly think that I have been overly cautious about this particular name. I very much dislike how expensive shares are on a price to book and price to tangible book basis. The fact that profitability is down year over year is discouraging as well. However, management exceeded analysts’ estimates on the top and bottom lines, and the balance sheet continues to expand. With no debt on its books either, I think that I have been wrong leading up to this point. As such, I have decided to upgrade it to a soft ‘buy’ at this time.

data.title: Euroapi: Public Financing, Reorganization, And Very Cheap
data.description:
Euroapi S.A. (EAPIF) recently announced financing from the European Commission as well as a plan to reorganize its business structure that may accelerate efficiency in the coming years. Operating in the growing active pharmaceutical ingredient market, and with existing facilities, Euroapi appears well positioned to grow in the coming years. There are some challenges coming from its relationship with Sanofi (SNY), which is the largest client. However, under my own financial models, I believe that the company could be trading at higher marks in the near future. According to Seeking Alpha, Euroapi trades at 3.9x FWD EBITDA.

Business Model, And Recent Guidance For 2025

Based in Europe, Euroapi produces pharmaceutical ingredients, and reports approximately 200 products in its portfolio. The company is a spin-off from Sanofi with 3k employees, and currently has a market capitalization of more than $300 million.

Euroapi reports six manufacturing sites in Europe, and the clients seem to receive products in 80 countries. According to the company's website, the company produces different API solutions including opiates, and controlled substances, vitamin B12, and hormones, among other substances.

According to market experts, the active pharmaceutical ingredient market is expected to grow at close to 6.9% CAGR from 2023 to 2032. Considering the market growth, I believe that Euroapi could experience substantial revenue growth and FCF growth in the next decade. Already with existing facilities and personnel, the company appears well positioned to benefit from the growth of the API market.

The global active pharmaceutical ingredient market size was USD 196.99 billion in 2023. The market is projected to grow from USD 209.80 billion in 2024 to USD 359.12 billion by 2032 at a CAGR of 6.9% over the forecast period. Source: Fortune Business Insights

I believe that it is a great moment for reviewing the company's business model considering the guidance given for 2025. The company expects business growth thanks to new customers other than Sanofi. In addition, the company decides to stop certain CMO projects that may not be that profitable. As a result, I believe that the company will most likely enhance future free cash flow margin growth in 2026 and 2027.

In 2024, the company reported a decline in revenue because of a large reduction in volume from Sanofi, and new agreements with Sanofi related to Global MSA clause. I am really not concerned because the company reported stable revenue and positive free cash flow in the past. In 2024, FCF was close to $137 million, and 2023 FCF was $107 million. I used some of these figures in my financial models.

In my view, the decline in the stock price represents a great opportunity to review the company's business model, and buy some shares. A few years ago, the shares traded at more than $21 per share. Right now, I am buying shares at $3-$4 per share.

Market expectations seem beneficial. Market analysts are expecting positive revenue growth from 2027 to 2034, and revenue growth in 2025 of 9%. The company trades at 0.2x 2034 sales, and 3.9x FWD EBITDA. In my view, Euroapi looks very undervalued.

Restructuring Costs Could Accelerate Free Cash Flow Growth From 2026

In the most recent annual report, management noted large restructuring costs of about €110 million and €120 million from 2024 to 2027. In my view, these efforts, in combination with the company's FOCUS-27 plan, will most likely bring significant efficiency and financial flexibility. As a result, we could see free cash flow margin improvements.

Despite the challenging global landscape in 2024, we have made progress in the company’s transformation. Source: Annual Report

It is worth noting that the company's FCF levered FCF margin TTM increased substantially as compared to that in 2023, 2022, and 2021. The company's restructuring plan seems to be working effectively. In my view, as soon as new investors revise the stock, I would be expecting demand for the stock.

Funding From The European Commission Could Accelerate The Demand For The Stock

Recently, the company reported that the European Commission selected Euroapi to receive $1 billion with the other 12 companies. In my view, the new financing will most likely enhance the company's balance sheet, and may attract the attention of new investors. Other large investors may decide to acquire shares because the European Commission is offering financing to the company. In sum, we could see demand for the stock in the coming years.

EUROAPI received official notification from the European Commission that it had been selected as one of the 13 companies eligible to share up to EUR 1 billion in total public funding under the Important Project of Common European Interest dedicated to the pharmaceutical sector. Source: Annual Report

It is also worth noting that a French government initiative called French Tech decided to buy 12% of Euroapi. Considering the amount of money received from the European Commission, and the French government, I would expect more investors to have a look at Euroapi in the future.

Best-Case Scenario Considering Funding From The European Commission, Restructuring Costs, And Market Growth

Under my best-case scenario, I assumed revenue growth close to 6.9% from 2027, and 2032 because of the expected growth of the pharmaceutical ingredient market. In addition, I used a FCF margin of 8% and 5.98%, which is not far from the figures reported by Euroapi, and other competitors.

I used FCF growth from $100 million in 2026 to $102 million in 2035. 2026 revenue would be $1.2 billion, and 2035 revenue would stand at $1.7 billion. According to Seeking Alpha, unlevered FCF was close to $137 million in 2024, $107.3 million in 2023, and $45.4 million in 2021. 2023 revenue was $1.1 billion, and 2022 revenue stood at $1.04 billion. In my view, my figures are in line with the figures reported by the company in the past.

Besides, with a WACC of 6% and a multiplier of 5x, I obtained a fair valuation of $11 per share. I took into account previous FCF numbers, the ongoing restructuring efforts, and the financing from the European Union.

The company appears to trade at 8x 2024 FCF, and 13x FWD cash flow. I used a multiplier of 5x FCF, which I think is quite conservative.

The CAPM figures reported by other investors in the market include a WACC of 6%-7%, a cost of equity of 6.6%-9%, and a tax rate of 35%-45% with beta close to 0.7x. In my view, my figures are not far from those of other analysts. I used a WACC of 6% with a beta of 0.7x.

Challenges From The Relationship With Sanofi

Sanofi is the company's main client, which is great because it is a massive client. However, I think that certain investors may be a bit concerned about the lack of diversification with regard to the number of clients. In the future, if the company delivers a large list of clients, and diminishes its relationship with Sanofi, I would expect new demand for the stock. There is something else. In my view, future negotiations of contracts with Sanofi could lead to a deterioration of the company's financial position. As a result, in the future, the company's operating margins could decline, which may lead to lower free cash flow growth and stock price declines.

Challenges For Investors In The United States Due To Changes In The US Dollar Value Against Euro

Euroapi operates mainly in Europe, and reports its figures in euros. Investors in the United States could suffer from an increase in the valuation of the US dollar as compared to the euro. Even if the company reports revenue growth and free cash flow growth, investors in the United States may lose money. I am not in a position to provide an opinion about the future of the EUR/USD exchange, so I believe that there is a clear risk here.

Challenges Coming From The War In Ukraine

Euroapi does not report any facility in Ukraine, however I believe that the war in Ukraine could have detrimental consequences in Europe. As a result, certain investors may decide to sell shares in Europe, and buy elsewhere. Debt capital markets in Europe could also suffer, which may not help Euroapi. Finally, the Euro could also lose some value, which may have an impact on the company's stock price.

Worst-Case Scenario Given Previous Challenges

Under my worst-case scenario, I assumed free cash flow ranging from $74 million in 2026 to approximately $38 million in 2035. Besides, I used revenue of $1 billion from 2026 to 2035, and a declining FCF margin from 7% in 2026 to 3.8% in 2035. I also used a WACC of 6%, 95 million shares outstanding, net debt of -$3.5 million, and a multiplier of 5x FCF. The results include an equity valuation of $534 million and a fair price of $5.6 per share.

In Conclusion: The Company Looks Like A Buy At 3.9x FWD EBITDA.

Euroapi operates in the growing API market, and reports existing facilities and well-known clients. The company appears to be in a process of reorganization, which was called FOCUS-27 plan, and noted a significant amount of restructuring costs expected in the coming years. With a large amount of debt, positive free cash flow, and recent funding from the European Commission, I do not think the market is paying a lot of attention. According to my DCF models, Euroapi appears substantially undervalued at the current price mark, and at 3.9x FWD EBITDA. As soon as more analysts have a look at the company, I believe that the demand for the stock will most likely trend north.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Etsy Q2 Earnings Preview: Leaning Towards A Bear Trap (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Etsy (ETSY), I rated the stock a “buy” with a price target of $73 per share.

At the time, Elliot Management had upped its stake in Etsy to account for 1.4% of its portfolio. Meanwhile, my bullish thesis on the stock was predicated on my belief that the initiatives that management was taking to revamp its app experience and improve its search algorithm, while testing a loyalty program in beta and introducing Etsy gift cards to physical stores, will translate into GMS (gross merchandise sales) and revenue growth accelerating from a higher number of active buyers and sellers.

While the stock has been up since the time of my writing, outperforming the S&P 500, I will, however, downgrade it at the moment, as revenue acceleration has not materialized despite management’s investments in products amid an uncertain macroeconomic environment and competitive pressures.

In Q1 FY25 earnings, GMS declined at an accelerating rate as active buyers and sellers on the platform continued to decline.

The company is set to report its Q2 FY25 earnings on 30th July, where management expects GMS to continue to decline at a similar rate to Q1, while full-year consensus estimates point to a revenue decline of -1.12% for the full year FY25.

The way I see it, the current projected growth rates in both revenue and earnings are not attractive enough to reignite investor optimism in the stock. Although the company is showcasing strength in its Services revenue, I believe that we are not going to see an expansion in the forward price-to-earnings multiple until management demonstrates signs of bottoming, followed by reacceleration in GMS with active buyers and sellers returning to the platform.

A Brief Recap Of Q1 FY25 Earnings

Etsy reported its Q1 FY25 earnings at the end of April, where total revenue grew just 0.8% YoY to $651M with a revenue take rate expansion of 170 basis points to 23.3%, along with adjusted EBITDA growing 1.9% YoY with a margin expansion of 30 basis points to 26.3%.

While both the top and bottom lines met analyst expectations, consolidated GMS was down 6.5% YoY, with Etsy marketplace GMS down 8.9% YoY to $2.3B, showing no signs of bottoming on a sequential basis.

The reason for the decline in GMS is tied to the number of both active buyers and sellers declining at an accelerated rate of 1.7% and 11.3% YoY, respectively, with GMS per active buyer declining 3.5% YoY to $120, despite efforts to reactivate users. This resulted in Marketplace revenue declining 1.8% YoY to $458.5M, contributing roughly 70% of Total Revenue.

As a result, the positive revenue growth was driven by growth in on-site advertising revenue for both Etsy and Depop, seller set-up fees, Payments expansion, with Services revenue growing 7.7% YoY, at an accelerated rate compared to the previous quarter, to $192.6M, contributing the remaining 30% of Total Revenue.

During the earnings call, the management cited macroeconomic uncertainty with the recent tariff announcements, the fluid state of consumer confidence, and a highly competitive environment as headwinds that are impacting the growth trajectory of the company.

Having said that, the company continues to invest in their product development pipeline, where they are focusing on leveraging AI and ML for deeper personalization and enhanced customer engagement with a new discovery-centric shopping experience launched on April 9. Meanwhile, the Etsy mobile app delivered its highest-ever share of GMS, at 44.5%, with an upward trend in both monthly active users and downloads from first-time app users.

However, looking forward, management guidance continues to remain muted, where they expect Q2 FY25 GMS to decline at a similar rate to Q1, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25%, leaving investor sentiment dampened in the stock as their product and marketing efforts show no signs of revenue reacceleration yet.

What To Look Forward To In Q2 FY25?

Etsy is set to report its Q2 FY25 on 30th July, where consensus estimates point to a total revenue of $647.7M, reflecting a revenue decline of 0.12% YoY, along with normalized earnings per share of $1.08.

Meanwhile, when it comes to forward consensus estimates for revenue and earnings growth beyond FY25, we can see early signs of bottoming for downward revisions on a 1M basis.

For instance, when it comes to revenue growth, while the 3- and 6M trends are negative, we can see that on a 1M basis, analysts have started revising their growth projections higher, with the company expected to see a revenue decline of -1.12% in FY25, followed by a recovery in the low to mid-single digits in the years after that.

On the other hand, the trend of forward revisions for earnings per share is murky, where analysts continue to revise their projections lower on a 1M basis for FY26 and FY27.

The thing is that the above growth rates are just not attractive enough, in my opinion, to initiate any reversal in investor sentiment at the moment.

The way I see it, Etsy’s growing Services revenue is a good sign, leading to a higher overall take rate and adjusted EBITDA expansion. However, in order to rebuild investor confidence in the stock, management needs to demonstrate bottoming, followed by a reacceleration in GMS, with active buyers and sellers returning to the platform.

Revisiting My Valuation

Taking forward consensus estimates for both revenue and earnings into account, we can see that Etsy should generate roughly $2.99B in revenue by FY27 with a normalized earnings per share of $5.54, which is equivalent to a present value of $4.48 when discounted at 8%.

While the stock is trading at just a multiple of 13x of its projected FY27 earnings per share, compared to the average S&P 500 multiple of 18x, I believe there is limited room for multiple expansions until the company can showcase signs of reacceleration, particularly in its top line.

Plus, looking at Wall Street price targets, we can see that the average price target stands at $52 per share at the moment, roughly 13% below where the stock is trading. Meanwhile, the difference between where the stock is currently trading versus the high and low price targets is less than 2%, which makes the risk-reward unattractive, in my opinion, to initiate a position at these levels.

As a result, I will downgrade the stock to a “hold” at the moment, with Wall Street’s average price target of $52 per share.

My Final Verdict And Conclusions

Despite the stock trading at a considerably low price-to-earnings multiple, I believe this company is currently a bear trap with insufficient risk-reward, at least until we see the decline in GMS find a bottom.

data.title: Take-Two Interactive: GTA VI May Crush It, But Taking Profits Is Wise
data.description:
I believe taking profits following Take-Two's (NASDAQ:TTWO) rally is wise, given the high idiosyncratic risk associated with GTA VI. Even if the game is a complete success, the stock already appears fully priced.

To understand my point, I believe it's first worth providing some context on what makes gaming stocks a challenging space to own in the first place.

See, as someone who loves video games, investing in video game stocks seemed appealing early in my investing career, until I quickly realized that making money with gaming stocks is very challenging.

A studio/publisher can make a banger game that will sell great for a year or two, but then you have to make another, as revenue will dry if you don't, and if you fail this time, you can potentially lose all the money you made the first time around, especially if you are looking for a bigger, more impressive sequel. It doesn't matter how many legendary titles you have released in the past.

Sure, they still generate a bit of revenue, but unless you keep development costs running and set to deliver another big title in the coming months/years, chances are no investor wants to hold your stock.

How can you hold a stock in the space for five or ten years, when you have no idea how well the 5-year down the line titles will be selling in what is also one of the most virtually competitive industries on Earth?

So it's no wonder that you see a successful company like Ubisoft (OTCPK:UBSFY) that has released legendary titles over the years has its stock trading at the same levels as it did 25 years ago.

As you can see in the table below, not only has the company's revenue not grown over the past decade, but with R&D as a percentage of revenues often above 40% or 50%, capital gets recycled in perpetuity in pursuit of the next expensive project that erodes any long-term prospects of actual value creation equity-wise.

Now, there are companies with more recurring revenue models, like Paradox Interactive’s (OTCPK:PRXXF) subscription-based DLC strategy for games like Cities: Skylines, which generates stable cash flows and leads to the majority of its revenue coming from DLCs and expansions.

As you can see in the table below, this time revenue growth is much more consistent, while operating margins often exceed 40%, way juicier than Ubisoft's thin, or even sometimes negative, operating margins.

The company has, in turn, been building equity value while paying out a sustainable and growing dividend.

Back to Take-Two, the company sits somewhere in the middle in terms of model, as it blends blockbuster releases like Grand Theft Auto with growing recurring revenue streams from GTA Online and mobile titles. Just to illustrate how big the company's titles are, its upcoming release, GTA VI, scheduled for May 26, 2026, is so highly anticipated that many competitors are deliberately avoiding launching titles around the same time, fearing that Rockstar’s giant title will completely dominate sales and consumer attention.

A $2 Billion Bet That Has Raised The Bar Too High

Now here's my problem. Yes, Take Two has historically exceeded expectations, so I do not fear that GTAVI will flop. My issue is with the fact that GTAVI has had a $2 billion development budget, and is thus one of the most ambitious entertainment projects ever, which has drained cash for too long.

Now, don't get me wrong, the delay from fall 2025 to May 2026 to ensure quality has generated unprecedented hype, with its 2nd trailer gathering 475 million views in 24 hours. GTA V’s success, with over 205 million units sold, sets a high bar, and yet Rockstar seems ready to impress us again. GTA VI is expected to employ a live-service model similar to GTA Online, which accounted for 77% of Take-Two’s net bookings from recurring consumer spending in the last quarter. GTA VI's online mode is expected to generate billions of dollars for years to come.

In the meantime, however, Take-Two has been pressuring the balance more and more in an attempt to keep funding this beast of an upcoming title. The company has gone from having a healthy net cash position to a net debt position of $2.64 billion.

The share count has also increased by about 140% over the past decade, with the company issuing tons of equity as an additional money-raising mechanism on of the strong cash flows their games have brought during the period.

And yes, the share count spike in 2022 was due to the acquisition of Zynga, but even as recently as May, TTWO announced a plan to offer $1 billion worth of shares, as apparently they need more liquidity, likely due to the final part of GTAVI's development phase needing more cash.

The point is that right now, GTAVI is a money pit, and so every cent of TTWO's share price entirely hinges on the expectation of the future sales of this very game. The bar has been set way too high, and even worse, even if GTAVI ends up being a great success, I believe the stock is mostly fully priced in already.

Why Taking Profits Might Be Smart

So even if GTA VI is an overwhelming commercial success, today's valuation of TTWO implies that much of that success is already priced in. If anything, I dare say that TTWO is quite overvalued.

According to DFC Intelligence, first-year sales projections for the game are staggering, with analysts expecting the title to generate anywhere between $3.2 billion and $7.6 billion in revenue during its first 12 months. This estimate basically assumes that Take-Two will sell 85 to 100 million copies of the game in its launch year, with an average unit price around $70 to $80, depending on region and edition tiers.

This is truly impressive as the company could literally recoup the entire $2 billion development cost within the first couple of months following release.

Looking beyond year one, expectations remain elevated with long-term forecasts suggesting GTA VI could sell 150 to 200 million copies over its lifecycle, with some outlier projections reaching 250 million, which would surpass even GTA V's record of 205 million units sold.

I believe the 250 million estimate is not unreasonable assuming we see GTAVI gets released on PlayStation 6, and who knows, PlayStation 7, in the same fashion GTAV got released over three generations of consoles.

Also, if GTA VI Online follows the model of GTA V Online, analysts expect it could generate $500 million to $1 billion annually in microtransactions and additional content for at least five to seven years. So more broadly, GTA VI could deliver $5–10 billion in online revenue over the next decade

However, I don't see how buying TTWO stock today is wise at a ~$43 billion market cap. The $3.2 billion to 7.6 billion estimate in sales means the company can repay its debt and build a cash position of $2-3 billion. So then you are looking at say a $2 billion annual contribution from GTAVI over the medium term from core game sales + online.

Now, even if all $2 billion ends up as an EBITDA contribution, along with the $400-500 million in EBITDA the company generates per year now, you get $2.5 billion in annual EBITDA. This implies a ~19X multiple on FY2026 EBITDA and, potentially, beyond. This is mostly in line with what consensus estimates show after the current year.

Still, this looks like a pricy multiple. Even if TTWO returns $2 billion a year to shareholders, you are looking at a capital return yield below 5% at the current share price, whether that's in the form of dividends or repurchases. TTWO may beat these estimates by, say, 10% or 20%, and yet, I don't see how the stock gains 20%, 30%, or 40% from here, as the valuation would expand to even more unreasonable levels. And remember, this is a company that by 2027 or 2028 will probably start developing the next title and start going through its next big development and R&D phase.

What To Do Now

In light of TTWO's fully priced valuation, I believe that taking profits here seems like a wise move. However, if you're reluctant to exit entirely, consider selling covered calls to generate income, as again, further share price gains seem somewhat unlikely.

For those not yet holding but interested in entering, you may want to consider selling out-of-the-money puts at a price where you'd feel comfortable owning the stock, and where it has a reasonable margin of safety as a smart, risk-adjusted strategy to gain exposure.
